title: Mindful life: Searching for wisdom in a time of tragedy
gist: A personal essay on grief drawn from a writer's mental-illness interview project, distilling a practical model for processing loss gradually and the compassion shown by the suicide-bereaved.

insights.claim: An interviewee's advice frames grief management as controlled release: 'You don’t open the tap all of a sudden so that it bursts out. You’ve got to let it out a little bit at a time.'
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: A memorable heuristic for grief processing that resonates in mental-health and bereavement contexts.
insights.quote: Dealing with grief, you’ve got to let it out in bits and pieces.
insights.locator: The words of one of these interviewees

insights.claim: Witnessing a loved one endure a shattering loss reveals that people summon unexpected inner resources and strength derived from their deep love for the person lost.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: An observation on human resilience under extreme loss.

insights.claim: People who have survived family members' suicides, interviewed for a mental-illness project, conveyed compassion that made the interviewer feel valued as a person in her own right.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Illustrates the lived-experience dimension behind mental-health and suicide-prevention themes.
insights.locator: The second revelation is about strangers

insights.claim: The author is working on a project about mental illness that involves interviewing people bereaved by suicide.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Signals ongoing editorial coverage of suicide bereavement at Prospect.
insights.locator: people I have interviewed for a project
